D9 BiologyL4 AnalyzeReference

Age-Related Yellowing — Lipofuscin Accumulation

aged skin and lens accumulate lipofuscin, a yellow-brown fluorescent pigment from lipid peroxidation; the eye lens specifically yellows enough to block blue light by age 70

Lipofuscin is an oxidatively cross-linked aggregate of lipid and protein fragments. Cells dispose of damaged proteins, but the residue accumulates with age. In the retinal pigment epithelium it absorbs blue → contributes to age-related macular degeneration. In the crystalline lens it gradually yellows; by 70 the lens transmits 60% less blue than at 20.

Drag age. Skin and lens swatches yellow; transmittance plot shows blue loss at high ages.
